---
title: "Theorem 5.5 — Gaussian law of the OLS estimator under Gaussian errors"
lean_name: "HansenEconometrics.olsBeta_hasGaussianLaw_of_error"
chapter: 5
---

## Textbook statement

> Paraphrased textbook statement (Hansen Theorem 5.5 / 5.6): in the
> homoskedastic normal regression model where $e \sim N(0, \sigma^2 I_n)$,
> the OLS estimator
>
> $$
> \hat\beta = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top y
> $$
>
> is jointly Gaussian, with mean $\beta$ and covariance $\sigma^2 (X^\top X)^{-1}$.
> Equivalently, $\hat\beta - \beta$ is the linear image of $e$ under
> $A := (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top$, so it inherits Gaussianity from $e$. The OLS
> residual vector $\hat e = M y$, where $M = I - X(X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top$, is
> also Gaussian, with mean $0$ and covariance $\sigma^2 M$.

In Lean, this is stated for a deterministic design $X$, coefficient vector
$\beta$, and random error vector $e$ under the
[Chapter 5 Gaussian-law assumptions](../../assumptions/chapter5.qmd):
`[Invertible (Xᵀ * X)]` and `HasGaussianLaw e μ`. Hansen's spherical
$e \sim N(0,\sigma^2 I)$ is a specialization; the formal theorem leaves the
mean and covariance abstract.

### Proof sketch

Substituting $y = X\beta + e$ into the closed form for $\hat\beta$,

$$
\hat\beta = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top (X\beta + e) = \beta + A e,
\qquad A := (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top.
$$

Since $A$ is a fixed (deterministic) linear map and $e$ is Gaussian, $Ae$
is Gaussian (linear images of Gaussians are Gaussian). Adding the
deterministic shift $\beta$ keeps it Gaussian (translations of Gaussians
are Gaussian). Hence $\hat\beta$ is Gaussian.

For the residual,

$$
\hat e = My = M(X\beta + e) = Me,
$$

because $MX = 0$. So $\hat e$ is the linear image $Me$, again Gaussian.

The whole argument is just the closure of Gaussianity under linear and
affine maps. Spectral arguments only appear later for the chi-square law in
Theorem 5.7.

## Lean statement

Main theorems in
[`HansenEconometrics/Chapter5NormalRegression.lean`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter5NormalRegression.lean):

- [`olsBeta_hasGaussianLaw_of_error`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter5NormalRegression.lean#L106)
  at `HansenEconometrics/Chapter5NormalRegression.lean:106` — the OLS
  coefficient vector inherits a Gaussian law from $e$.
- [`residual_hasGaussianLaw_of_error`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter5NormalRegression.lean#L135)
  at `HansenEconometrics/Chapter5NormalRegression.lean:135` — the OLS
  residual vector inherits a Gaussian law from $e$.

The headline theorem reads:

::: {.lean-hover-panel}
```{=html}
<pre><code><span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="Headline Gaussian-closure theorem for the OLS coefficient vector.">theorem olsBeta_hasGaussianLaw_of_error</span>
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="Underlying sample space, measurable structure, and measure.">{Ω : Type*} [MeasurableSpace Ω] {μ : Measure Ω}</span>
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="Deterministic design X, true coefficient β, and random error vector e.">(X : Matrix n k ℝ) (β : k → ℝ) (e : Ω → n → ℝ)</span>
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="Full-rank condition for the OLS closed form.">[Invertible (Xᵀ * X)]</span>
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="Substantive probabilistic hypothesis: the error vector has a Gaussian law under μ.">(he : HasGaussianLaw e μ)</span> :
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="Conclusion: the OLS estimator computed from y = Xβ + e is itself Gaussian.">HasGaussianLaw (fun ω => olsBeta X (X *ᵥ β + e ω)) μ</span>
</code></pre>
```
:::

### Reading the statement

In English: **given** an underlying probability space $(\Omega, \mu)$, a
deterministic design matrix $X$, a true coefficient vector $\beta$, a random
error vector $e : \Omega \to \mathbb{R}^n$ with a Gaussian law under $\mu$, and
a witness that $X^\top X$ is invertible, the result is that the random variable
$\omega \mapsto \hat\beta\bigl(X(X\beta + e(\omega))\bigr)$ also has a Gaussian
law under $\mu$.

| Lean | Math | What it gives us |
|---|---|---|
| `{Ω : Type*}` | $\Omega$ | **Given:** the underlying sample space. Implicit; Lean infers it from the use site. |
| `[MeasurableSpace Ω]` | $\sigma$-algebra on $\Omega$ | **Given (instance):** the measurable structure on $\Omega$. Mathlib boilerplate so that `Measure Ω` makes sense. |
| `{μ : Measure Ω}` | $\mu$ on $\Omega$ | **Given:** the probability measure. Implicit — supplied at the use site. (Mathlib `Measure` is general; the Gaussian-law hypothesis is what makes it behave like a probability measure for this argument.) |
| `(X : Matrix n k ℝ)` | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times k}$ | **Given:** deterministic design matrix. Index types `n` (observations) and `k` (regressors) come from the section variables. |
| `(β : k → ℝ)` | $\beta \in \mathbb{R}^k$ | **Given:** true coefficient vector. A Mathlib real vector is a function `k → ℝ`. |
| `(e : Ω → n → ℝ)` | $e : \Omega \to \mathbb{R}^n$ | **Given:** random error vector. For each $\omega \in \Omega$, $e(\omega)$ is a vector in $\mathbb{R}^n$. |
| `[Invertible (Xᵀ * X)]` | $X^\top X$ invertible | **Given (instance):** Hansen's full-column-rank assumption, packaged as a typeclass instance so `olsBeta X · ` elaborates. |
| `(he : HasGaussianLaw e μ)` | $e$ has a Gaussian law under $\mu$ | **Given (hypothesis):** the substantive probabilistic assumption. The pushforward $\mu \circ e^{-1}$ is a Gaussian measure on $\mathbb{R}^n$. Mean and covariance are not pinned down — this is more general than Hansen's $e \sim N(0, \sigma^2 I)$. |
| `HasGaussianLaw (fun ω => olsBeta X (X *ᵥ β + e ω)) μ` | $\hat\beta$ is Gaussian under $\mu$ | **Conclusion:** under $\mu$, the random variable $\omega \mapsto \hat\beta\bigl(X(X\beta + e(\omega))\bigr)$ has a Gaussian law on $\mathbb{R}^k$. |

(`X *ᵥ β` is `Matrix.mulVec`, so `X *ᵥ β + e ω` is the linear-model
realization $X\beta + e(\omega)$, and `olsBeta X (X *ᵥ β + e ω)` is the OLS
estimator computed on that realized $y$.)

## Translation notes

- Hansen states $\hat\beta \sim N(\beta, \sigma^2(X^\top X)^{-1})$. Lean
  states `HasGaussianLaw (fun ω => olsBeta X (X *ᵥ β + e ω)) μ` — that is,
  $\hat\beta$ inherits a Gaussian law from $e$. The exact mean and
  covariance can be read off from the linear decomposition theorem
  ([`olsBeta_linear_decomposition`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter4LeastSquaresRegression.lean#L16)
  from Chapter 4) but isn't packaged into a single statement here.
- The hypothesis `HasGaussianLaw e μ` is the
  Mathlib / probability-theory notion that the pushforward of $e$ under
  $\mu$ is a Gaussian measure on $\mathbb{R}^n$. This is exactly the
  textbook hypothesis $e \sim N(\cdot, \cdot)$, with parametric mean and
  covariance abstracted away.
- The proof goes through `Matrix.toLin'` to package the linear map $A$ as a
  `ContinuousLinearMap`, then uses `HasGaussianLaw.map_fun` together with
  the affine-shift lemma for Gaussian measures.

## Tactic-by-tactic walkthrough

Hover over a proof line to read how the Gaussian-closure argument is assembled.

::: {.lean-hover-panel}
```{=html}
<pre><code><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="The coefficient theorem: if the error vector is Gaussian, then the OLS coefficient computed from Xβ + e is Gaussian.">theorem olsBeta_hasGaussianLaw_of_error
    {Ω : Type*} [MeasurableSpace Ω] {μ : Measure Ω}
    (X : Matrix n k ℝ) (β : k → ℝ) (e : Ω → n → ℝ)
    [Invertible (Xᵀ * X)]
    (he : HasGaussianLaw e μ) :
    HasGaussianLaw (fun ω => olsBeta X (X *ᵥ β + e ω)) μ := by</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Package A = (X'X)⁻¹X' as a continuous linear map, the form expected by Mathlib's Gaussian-law API.">  let L : (n → ℝ) →L[ℝ] (k → ℝ) :=
    (Matrix.toLin' (⅟ (Xᵀ * X) * Xᵀ)).toContinuousLinearMap</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Linear images of Gaussian random vectors are Gaussian, so Ae is Gaussian.">  have hLin : HasGaussianLaw (fun ω => L (e ω)) μ := he.map_fun L</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Build the affine-shift version β + Ae. This is the Gaussian variable that the OLS decomposition will identify with β-hat.">  have hAff : HasGaussianLaw (fun ω => β + L (e ω)) μ := by</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Open HasGaussianLaw to prove the pushed-forward measure is Gaussian.">    refine ⟨?_⟩</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Show that mapping μ by L∘e and then translating by β is the same pushforward as mapping directly by ω ↦ β + L(eω).">    have hmap : (μ.map fun ω => L (e ω)).map (fun x => β + x) = μ.map (fun ω => β + L (e ω)) := by
      simpa using
        (AEMeasurable.map_map_of_aemeasurable
          (μ := μ)
          (f := fun ω => L (e ω))
          (g := fun x => β + x)
          (Measurable.aemeasurable <| by fun_prop)
          hLin.aemeasurable)</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Switch to the two-step pushforward form so the affine-shift Gaussian instance can apply.">    rw [← hmap]</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Register that the inner pushforward, the law of Ae, is Gaussian.">    letI : IsGaussian (μ.map fun ω => L (e ω)) := hLin.isGaussian_map</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Mathlib's affine-shift instance now proves the translated measure is Gaussian.">    infer_instance</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="It remains to identify β + Ae with the actual OLS estimator almost everywhere.">  refine hAff.congr ?_</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Reduce the almost-everywhere equality to a pointwise equality at an arbitrary sample point.">  filter_upwards with ω</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Use the deterministic OLS decomposition β-hat(Xβ+e) = β + (X'X)⁻¹X'e.">  rw [olsBeta_linear_decomposition]</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Unfold L and let simplification match the continuous-linear-map packaging with the matrix-vector expression.">  simp [L]</span>
</code></pre>
```
:::

For the residual,
[`residual_hasGaussianLaw_of_error`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter5NormalRegression.lean#L135)
is much shorter — there is no affine shift, only a linear map:

::: {.lean-hover-panel}
```{=html}
<pre><code><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Residual theorem: the residual from Xβ + e is Gaussian because it is Me.">theorem residual_hasGaussianLaw_of_error
    {Ω : Type*} [MeasurableSpace Ω] {μ : Measure Ω}
    (X : Matrix n k ℝ) (β : k → ℝ) (e : Ω → n → ℝ)
    [Invertible (Xᵀ * X)]
    (he : HasGaussianLaw e μ) :
    HasGaussianLaw (fun ω => residual X (X *ᵥ β + e ω)) μ := by</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Package the annihilator M as a continuous linear map on Rⁿ.">  let L : (n → ℝ) →L[ℝ] (n → ℝ) := (Matrix.toLin' (annihilatorMatrix X)).toContinuousLinearMap</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Linear images of Gaussian vectors are Gaussian, so Me is Gaussian.">  have hLin : HasGaussianLaw (fun ω => L (e ω)) μ := he.map_fun L</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="It remains to identify Me with the actual OLS residual almost everywhere.">  refine hLin.congr ?_</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Reduce the almost-everywhere equality to a pointwise residual identity.">  filter_upwards with ω</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Use the deterministic identity residual(X, Xβ+e) = Me.">  rw [residual_linear_model]</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Unfold L to match the annihilator matrix-vector expression.">  simp [L]</span>
</code></pre>
```
:::

## Downstream uses

- The chi-square / independence theorem (Hansen Theorem 5.7): once
  $\hat\beta$ and $\hat e$ are known to be jointly Gaussian, independence
  reduces to a covariance computation, and that cross-covariance is zero
  because $X^\top M = 0$.
- Confidence intervals and t-tests in Chapter 5 build on this Gaussianity
  of $\hat\beta$.
- More generally, any normal-error inference statement for OLS that
  doesn't go through asymptotics depends on this finite-sample Gaussian
  law.
